task_description:
Screen each abstract for relevance to machine learning for predicting sepsis onset or progression in intensive care. Rate methodological quality.

Relevance: RELEVANT if the paper applies ML to predict sepsis onset, septic shock, organ failure, or response to sepsis treatment, using any ICU or clinical data. NOT_RELEVANT if the clinical outcome is not sepsis-related, or if no ML is applied.

Quality rubric:
  1 = No methodology or purely conceptual.
  2 = Approach mentioned but key details absent (no dataset size, no metrics, no comparison).
  3 = Clear methodology with evaluation metrics and dataset; limited or no baseline.
  4 = Full methodology, multiple baselines, external validation or ablation, and reproducibility.

papers.title: The Potential of AI for Sepsis Management in the ICU
papers.abstract: Sepsis remains the leading cause of ICU mortality. Artificial intelligence may help through earlier detection, better resuscitation guidance, and personalised antibiotic selection. This editorial discusses the promise and challenges without reporting original data or evaluation.
papers.methodology_hint: editorial, no model, no data, no metrics
papers.claimed_contribution: Editorial on AI potential for ICU sepsis management.

papers.title: Temporal Convolutional Network for Sepsis Onset Prediction from Continuous Vital Streams
papers.abstract: Continuous physiological monitoring in the ICU generates high-frequency data that sequential models can exploit for early warning. We train a temporal convolutional network on 6-hour rolling windows of HR, MAP, SpO2, and temperature from 48,000 MIMIC-IV admissions to predict sepsis onset 6 hours before clinical recognition. AUROC 0.87, sensitivity 0.81, and specificity 0.84 are achieved. Comparison against NEWS2, qSOFA, and an LSTM baseline is provided, along with an ablation of input feature sets.
papers.topic_hint: TCN, sepsis onset, MIMIC-IV, vital signs, early warning
papers.methodology_hint: TCN, MIMIC-IV 48k, AUROC 0.87, NEWS2/qSOFA/LSTM baselines, feature ablation
papers.claimed_contribution: TCN for 6-hour-early sepsis prediction AUROC 0.87, 3 clinical and ML baselines, ablation.

papers.title: Random Forest for Predicting Sepsis in the Emergency Department
papers.abstract: We applied machine learning to ED presentations to identify sepsis. Random forest was trained on a single-centre dataset of 8,000 patients. AUC was good. Clinical features were important. More work is needed.
papers.methodology_hint: random forest, 8k patients, vague AUC, no specific metrics, no baseline, inadequate reporting
papers.claimed_contribution: Random forest for ED sepsis prediction; results vaguely reported.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning for Personalised Vasopressor Dosing in Septic Shock
papers.abstract: Vasopressor titration in septic shock involves complex trade-offs. We train a deep RL agent on 8,000 septic shock episodes from MIMIC-III to recommend vasopressor doses and fluid administration. The policy reduces mortality risk by 14% compared to clinician behaviour in offline policy evaluation. We compare against behaviour cloning and doubly-robust policy evaluation methods.
papers.topic_hint: reinforcement learning, vasopressor, septic shock, MIMIC-III, treatment
papers.methodology_hint: deep RL, MIMIC-III 8k episodes, 14% mortality reduction, behaviour cloning comparison
papers.claimed_contribution: RL vasopressor policy for septic shock, 14% mortality reduction, 2 comparison methods.

papers.title: XGBoost for Predicting Sepsis-Related Acute Kidney Injury Within 48 Hours
papers.abstract: Sepsis-related acute kidney injury (SA-AKI) is a common and serious complication. We train an XGBoost model on 22,000 septic admissions from eICU-CRD to predict SA-AKI onset within 48 hours using lab values, fluids, and vasopressor exposure. AUROC 0.81 with good calibration is achieved. We compare against logistic regression and SOFA score but no other ML models are tested.
papers.topic_hint: XGBoost, sepsis, AKI, eICU, lab values
papers.methodology_hint: XGBoost, eICU 22k, AUROC 0.81, LR and SOFA comparison — no other ML baselines
papers.claimed_contribution: XGBoost for SA-AKI prediction, AUROC 0.81, LR and SOFA comparison, eICU-CRD.

papers.title: Deep Learning for Automated ECG Rhythm Classification in Cardiac Monitoring
papers.abstract: We train a 1D convolutional network on 300,000 ECG recordings to classify 12 arrhythmia types. AUC 0.97 is achieved, outperforming cardiologist consensus. This is a cardiac arrhythmia classification task; sepsis detection or ICU outcome prediction is not addressed.
papers.topic_hint: CNN, ECG, arrhythmia classification, cardiac monitoring
papers.methodology_hint: 1D CNN, 300k ECGs, AUC 0.97, cardiologist comparison — cardiac not sepsis
papers.claimed_contribution: 1D CNN for arrhythmia classification AUC 0.97, outperforming cardiologists, not sepsis-related.

papers.title: Transformer Encoder for 24-Hour Sepsis Mortality Prediction Using Multivariate ICU Time-Series
papers.abstract: We fine-tune a transformer encoder on multivariate ICU time-series — labs, vitals, medications, and infusions — from 31,000 MIMIC-IV sepsis admissions to predict 24-hour in-hospital mortality. AUROC 0.88 is achieved. We compare against LSTM, TCN, and a logistic regression baseline. An ablation confirms that medication and infusion features add 4 points above vitals+labs alone. Model weights and processing code are released.
papers.topic_hint: transformer, sepsis mortality, MIMIC-IV, multivariate time-series
papers.methodology_hint: transformer, MIMIC-IV 31k, AUROC 0.88, LSTM/TCN/LR baselines, ablation, code released
papers.claimed_contribution: Transformer for sepsis 24-hour mortality, AUROC 0.88, 3 baselines, ablation, code released.

papers.title: Predicting Flu Epidemic Severity Using Social Media and Surveillance Data
papers.abstract: We combine Twitter health mentions and CDC influenza surveillance data to predict weekly flu epidemic severity at state level. An LSTM achieves MAE 0.8 cases per 100,000 people. This forecasting model targets influenza epidemiology, not sepsis or ICU patient outcomes.
papers.topic_hint: LSTM, influenza, Twitter, surveillance, epidemic forecasting
papers.methodology_hint: LSTM, Twitter + CDC, flu forecasting — influenza epidemiology not sepsis
papers.claimed_contribution: LSTM for flu severity forecasting from social media and surveillance data, MAE 0.8.

papers.title: Gradient Boosting for Predicting Sepsis Response to Initial Antibiotics at 72 Hours
papers.abstract: Identifying patients unlikely to respond to initial antibiotics early could guide timely de-escalation. We train gradient boosting on 14,000 septic episodes from two hospital systems using clinical features at 24 hours post-admission. AUROC 0.76 for non-response at 72 hours is achieved. An external validation cohort at a third site confirms generalisability.
papers.topic_hint: gradient boosting, sepsis, antibiotic response, de-escalation, external validation
papers.methodology_hint: gradient boosting, 14k episodes, 2 hospitals, AUROC 0.76, external validation — no ML baselines
papers.claimed_contribution: Gradient boosting for sepsis antibiotic non-response prediction, AUROC 0.76, external validation.

papers.title: Protein Language Model for Bacteriophage Host Range Prediction
papers.abstract: Predicting which bacterial hosts a phage can infect is important for phage therapy applications. We fine-tune a protein language model on phage tail fibre protein sequences to predict host range across 12 genera. AUC 0.83 is achieved. No ICU data or sepsis outcome is involved.
papers.topic_hint: protein language model, bacteriophage, host range prediction, phage therapy
papers.methodology_hint: protein LM, phage tail fibres, AUC 0.83 — phage biology not sepsis
papers.claimed_contribution: Protein LM for phage host range prediction, AUC 0.83, phage therapy application.
